questions.stem: Which hypothesis should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: knowledge deficit as the only active problem
stable chronic disease without acute change
interrupted cerebral perfusion from arterial occlusion
mild symptoms that can wait for scheduled reassessment

distractorRationales.d: "mild symptoms that can wait for scheduled reassessment" is lower priority because the current cue pattern most strongly threatens interrupted cerebral perfusion from arterial occlusion.

questions.takeaway: Prioritize interrupted cerebral perfusion from arterial occlusion.

exhibits.body: Nutrition support began yesterday and the client now reports weakness. Fatigue, paresthesias, mild confusion, and irregular pulse.

exhibits.title: Assessment
items: Fatigue, paresthesias, mild confusion, and irregular pulse.
severe hypophosphatemia after nutrition is restarted

questions.stem: Which hypothesis should the nurse prioritize?

options.text: stable chronic disease without acute change
insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction
mild symptoms that can wait for scheduled reassessment
knowledge deficit as the only active problem

questions.rationale: insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction is the priority hypothesis because it creates the most immediate risk in the scenario. Lower-acuity explanations do not account for the unstable cue pattern.

distractorRationales.a: "stable chronic disease without acute change" is lower priority because the current cue pattern most strongly threatens insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction.
distractorRationales.c: "mild symptoms that can wait for scheduled reassessment" is lower priority because the current cue pattern most strongly threatens insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction.
distractorRationales.d: "knowledge deficit as the only active problem" is lower priority because the current cue pattern most strongly threatens insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction.

questions.takeaway: Prioritize insulin-driven intracellular electrolyte shifts after carbohydrate reintroduction.

exhibits.title: Assessment
items: Fatigue, paresthesias, mild confusion, and irregular pulse.
severe hypophosphatemia after nutrition is restarted

questions.stem: Which solution best addresses the priority hypothesis?

options.text: replace electrolytes and slow caloric advancement with close monitoring
continue routine observation and reassess during the next scheduled rounds
focus on comfort measures before treating the underlying refeeding syndrome with dysrhythmia and respiratory muscle risk
begin discharge teaching and outpatient follow-up planning

questions.rationale: The best solution is to replace electrolytes and slow caloric advancement with close monitoring. This matches the active physiology and avoids delaying stabilization for comfort, teaching, or routine reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: "continue routine observation and reassess during the next scheduled rounds" does not address the immediate physiology; the safer plan is to replace electrolytes and slow caloric advancement with close monitoring.
distractorRationales.c: "focus on comfort measures before treating the underlying refeeding syndrome with dysrhythmia and respiratory muscle risk" does not address the immediate physiology; the safer plan is to replace electrolytes and slow caloric advancement with close monitoring.
distractorRationales.d: "begin discharge teaching and outpatient follow-up planning" does not address the immediate physiology; the safer plan is to replace electrolytes and slow caloric advancement with close monitoring.

questions.takeaway: Generate a solution that treats the physiologic risk first.

questions.scenario: A 19-year-old client admitted for anorexia nervosa. Nutrition support began yesterday and the client now reports weakness. Very low intake for several weeks and 18% weight loss.

exhibits.title: Nurses' note
exhibits.body: Nutrition support began yesterday and the client now reports weakness. Fatigue, paresthesias, mild confusion, and irregular pulse.

exhibits.title: Assessment
items: Fatigue, paresthesias, mild confusion, and irregular pulse.
severe hypophosphatemia after nutrition is restarted

questions.stem: Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: rapidly increasing calories because weight restoration is urgent
document the findings and recheck after routine medications
ask the client to ambulate to clarify symptom severity
notify the provider and prepare electrolyte replacement before advancing calories

questions.rationale: The first action is to notify the provider and prepare electrolyte replacement before advancing calories. rapidly increasing calories because weight restoration is urgent would place a lower-priority task ahead of stabilization and escalation.

distractorRationales.a: "rapidly increasing calories because weight restoration is urgent" can delay stabilization; the nurse should first notify the provider and prepare electrolyte replacement before advancing calories.
distractorRationales.b: "document the findings and recheck after routine medications" can delay stabilization; the nurse should first notify the provider and prepare electrolyte replacement before advancing calories.
distractorRationales.c: "ask the client to ambulate to clarify symptom severity" can delay stabilization; the nurse should first notify the provider and prepare electrolyte replacement before advancing calories.

questions.takeaway: Take action before the risk progresses.

questions.stem: Which finding best indicates that the intervention is effective?

options.text: one noncritical lab value is unchanged
the family verbalizes understanding of teaching
phosphorus, potassium, and rhythm stabilize
the client states they feel tired but wants to sleep

questions.rationale: The best evaluation marker is that phosphorus, potassium, and rhythm stabilize. It directly shows whether the priority physiology is improving rather than only measuring comfort or teaching.

distractorRationales.a: "one noncritical lab value is unchanged" is not the best outcome marker because improvement should show that phosphorus, potassium, and rhythm stabilize.
distractorRationales.b: "the family verbalizes understanding of teaching" is not the best outcome marker because improvement should show that phosphorus, potassium, and rhythm stabilize.
distractorRationales.d: "the client states they feel tired but wants to sleep" is not the best outcome marker because improvement should show that phosphorus, potassium, and rhythm stabilize.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate the outcome tied to the original safety threat.
